atoms.content: A Decision Tree classifies data by answering a series of sequential Yes/No questions, splitting the dataset into smaller branches until it reaches a final decision.
It works exactly like a game of '20 Questions'. Does it have fur? (Yes) Does it bark? (Yes) -> It's a dog.
The tree decides which question to ask first by using math (Gini Impurity or Entropy) to find the question that most perfectly separates the data categories.
Decision trees are highly prone to overfitting because, if left unconstrained, they will keep asking questions until every single training data point has its own tiny branch.

1. Highly interpretable.
2. No data scaling required.
3. Extremely prone to overfitting if not pruned.
Does a Decision Tree require you to use Min-Max scaling on your features before training? (Answer: No, trees do not rely on distances between points).
